chunk_summary: The 24-hour recall multi-pass method is a structured dietary assessment tool to estimate intake, identify gaps, and guide nutrition support, especially in children and infants, with strengths in detail and limitations in variability and recall bias.
learning_objectives: Understand the multi-pass methodology for 24-hour dietary recall and its application in clinical settings.
Identify strengths and limitations of the 24-hour recall method, including recall bias and day-to-day variability.
Learn strategies to improve recall accuracy, such as utensil prompts and standardized household measures.
Explore the use of Indian Food Composition Tables (IFCT) for nutrient analysis in dietary assessments.
key_facts: The 24-hour recall involves a multi-pass method: free listing, detailed probing, forgotten item checks, and summary confirmation.
Infant-specific considerations include breastfeeding frequency, complementary food texture, and caregiver feeding roles.
Day-to-day variability limits single-day recall; repeat on 2–3 non-consecutive days for persistent issues like growth faltering.
Portion estimation errors can be reduced using utensil prompts and standardized household measure guides.
Indian Food Composition Tables (IFCT) and NIN data are recommended for nutrient analysis in Indian contexts.
reference_quote: The USDA Automated Multiple-Pass Method for 24-hour dietary recall is a validated tool to improve recall completeness and accuracy.
document_key_topics: Strengths and limitations of dietary recall
Nutrient analysis using Indian food composition data
